description:
You are an edge-computing engineer preparing a checksum index that will be shipped
    together with every configuration bundle for a fleet of IoT devices.

    Current workspace
    -----------------
    1. Directory tree
       /home/user/device_configs/
       ├── site_a/
       │   ├── gateway.cfg
       │   └── sensor1.cfg
       └── site_b/
           └── sensor2.cfg

    2. File contents  
       a) /home/user/device_configs/site_a/gateway.cfg  
          gateway_id=GW-001  
          ip_address=192.168.1.1  
          port=1883  

       b) /home/user/device_configs/site_a/sensor1.cfg  
          sensor_id=S-100  
          frequency=30  
          unit=celsius  

       c) /home/user/device_configs/site_b/sensor2.cfg  
          sensor_id=S-200  
          frequency=15  
          unit=humidity  

       (Every line shown above, including the last one in each file, is terminated
       with a single Unix newline character “\n”.)

    Task
    ----
    Create a file called  
        /home/user/device_configs/config_checksums.txt  
    that lists an MD5 checksum for every “.cfg” file under
        /home/user/device_configs
    – no matter how deeply it is nested.

    • Use exactly the same textual format that the standard GNU **md5sum** utility
      produces when fed a list of files on its standard input:

          <32-hex-digit-checksum><two spaces><absolute-path>

      Example of the required layout (hex values obviously differ):

          d41d8cd98f00b204e9800998ecf8427e  /full/path/to/example.cfg

    • Each of the three configuration files must appear on its own line.
    • Lines may appear in any order; duplicates are not allowed.
    • There must be **no additional text** in the file and **no trailing “total” line**.
    • A single, correctly-constructed pipeline that combines **find** and **xargs**
      (together with whatever checksum utility you prefer) is sufficient to finish
      the job.

    Once you have run the command, confirm that
        /home/user/device_configs/config_checksums.txt  
    exists and strictly follows the format above.  The automated checker will:

    1. Verify that the file exists and is not empty.  
    2. Read every line, split it on the two-space separator, re-compute the MD5 of
       the referenced *.cfg* file, and compare the results.  
    3. Ensure that exactly three, and only three, correct lines are present.

    Do **not** hard-code the checksums; generate them automatically from the actual
    files with **find** + **xargs** in a single command pipeline.
truth:
Initial directory structure that exists when the task starts:

    /home/user/device_configs/
    ├── site_a/
    │   ├── gateway.cfg          (three lines, see task description)
    │   └── sensor1.cfg          (three lines, see task description)
    └── site_b/
        └── sensor2.cfg          (three lines, see task description)

    Exact textual contents (each line ends with “\n”):

    1. /home/user/device_configs/site_a/gateway.cfg
       gateway_id=GW-001\n
       ip_address=192.168.1.1\n
       port=1883\n

    2. /home/user/device_configs/site_a/sensor1.cfg
       sensor_id=S-100\n
       frequency=30\n
       unit=celsius\n

    3. /home/user/device_configs/site_b/sensor2.cfg
       sensor_id=S-200\n
       frequency=15\n
       unit=humidity\n

    The checker will dynamically compute the MD5 digests of these three files.
    It will then verify that:

      • /home/user/device_configs/config_checksums.txt is present.
      • The file contains exactly three non-blank lines.
      • Every line matches the regular expression
            ^[0-9a-f]{32}  /home/user/device_configs/.+\.cfg$
      • For each line, the 32-hex digest equals the MD5 of the referenced file.
      • No extra lines (e.g., a “total” line) exist.

    The agent does not need root privileges and has full write permissions in
    /home/user and all its sub-directories.
